Ayah 1–8 · linked video: 02. Al-Fath (Ayah 1-8)

Surah Al-Fath: Commentary on Ayahs 1-8

Ayah 1

إِنَّا فَتَحْنَا لَكَ فَتْحًا مُّبِينًا

“Indeed, We have granted you a clear victory.”

The opening declaration of Surah Al-Fath addresses one of the most significant yet misunderstood events in Islamic history: the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. When Allah ﷻ revealed these words, declaring “We have given you a clear, open victory,” the immediate context might seem puzzling. The Muslims had just returned from what appeared to be a frustrating journey. They set out with the intention of performing pilgrimage, yet were prevented from entering Makkah and instead negotiated a treaty that seemed, on the surface, to favor the Quraysh.

To understand the weight of this divine declaration, we must examine the strategic implications of Hudaybiyyah. Before this treaty, the Muslims faced enemies on multiple fronts. The Quraysh in Makkah represented their primary opposition, but they also had to contend with Jewish tribes who had broken their treaties and established a fortified base in Khaybar. These forces were connected—the Jews had ties with Makkah, and any military engagement with one could trigger retaliation from the other.

The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah fundamentally changed this equation. By establishing a ten-year peace agreement with the Quraysh, Allah ﷻ severed the strategic alliance between Makkah and the Jewish forces. The Muslims could no longer be threatened from multiple directions simultaneously. This diplomatic victory opened a clear path forward that would prove far more effective than any military engagement could have achieved at that moment.

Moreover, the treaty began to isolate Makkah economically and politically. The Quraysh had relied on various tribal alliances and trade relationships that extended in every direction—to Syria in the north, to Iraq in the east, and to Yemen in the south. The establishment of peace with the Muslims, ironically, began to erode these other connections. The sphere of Islamic influence started expanding, and Makkah’s traditional power base began to contract. The margins were shrinking, and the Quraysh found themselves increasingly boxed in, unable to seek help even from the people of Yemen, who harbored their own grievances against the Makkans over trade disputes.

The word فَتْحًا carries profound significance. While commonly translated as “victory” or “conquest,” it fundamentally means “opening.” Allah ﷻ opened a pathway that would lead directly to the conquest of Makkah without the complications and uncertainties that military confrontation would have entailed. This was a victory of strategic positioning, of divine wisdom working through what appeared to be setback and compromise.

Ayah 2

لِّيَغْفِرَ لَكَ ٱللَّهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِن ذَنۢبِكَ وَمَا تَأَخَّرَ وَيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكَ وَيَهْدِيَكَ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا

“So that Allah may forgive you what preceded of your sin and what will follow, and complete His favor upon you, and guide you to a straight path.”

This ayah begins with لِّيَغْفِرَ , “so that He may forgive,” directly connecting the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah to divine forgiveness. On first reading, this connection might seem puzzling. Why would a political treaty relate to the Prophet’s ﷺ forgiveness? The answer lies in understanding what ذَنۢب means in this prophetic context.

When Allah ﷻ speaks of the Prophet’s ﷺ dhanb, He is not referring to sins in the conventional sense that we understand for ourselves. Rather, this refers to strategic decisions—judgments made at the human level that, while representing the best possible choice with available information, could only be perfected by divine intervention. These are the ijtihad decisions that a prophet makes in his role as a political and military leader, decisions where multiple valid options exist and only Allah ﷻ knows with certainty which path will lead to the ultimate success.

By saying “what preceded of your dhanb and what will follow,” Allah ﷻ is providing the Prophet ﷺ with a comprehensive reassurance. Whatever strategic decisions he made in the past that he might question in hindsight, and whatever decisions he would need to make in the future—none of these would hamper the inevitable march toward victory. From Hudaybiyyah forward, the path to Makkah would be a straight projectile. The domino effect had been set in motion, and divine wisdom would override any human uncertainty in judgment.

The phrase وَيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكَ , “and complete His favor upon you,” carries extraordinary significance when we remember the prayer of Prophet Ibrahim (AS). When Ibrahim (AS) raised the foundations of the Ka’bah with his son Isma’il (AS), he made a supplication that included the words: “Our Lord, and send among them a messenger from themselves who will recite to them Your verses and teach them the Book and wisdom and purify them” (2:129).

The completion of Allah’s ﷻ favor upon the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ involves the fulfillment of Ibrahim’s (AS) du’a. How would the Prophet ﷺ truly purify the people when the House of Allah ﷻ itself remained impure, filled with idols and controlled by those who had corrupted its purpose? The completion of the favor would come through the purification of the Ka’bah—through its return to the pure monotheistic worship for which it was originally built. Hudaybiyyah set in motion the chain of events that would make this possible.

The ayah concludes with وَيَهْدِيَكَ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا , “and guide you to a straight path.” This requires careful reflection. Had the Prophet ﷺ not been on a straight path for the previous nineteen years of his prophethood—thirteen years in Makkah and six in Madinah? Of course he had been guided. But this is not speaking about the straight path of spiritual guidance; this is the straight path to victory, the direct route to Makkah. Allah ﷻ is declaring that from this point forward, there would be no more complications, no more obstacles that could prevent the ultimate triumph. The path ahead was clear and straight.

Notice how Allah ﷻ connects these concepts with the opening لِّ , “so that.” The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah was orchestrated so that these strategic concerns would be resolved, so that the path forward would be clear, so that the ultimate victory would be guaranteed. Each element flows from the divine wisdom behind what appeared to be a compromising treaty.

Ayah 3

وَيَنصُرَكَ ٱللَّهُ نَصْرًا عَزِيزًا

“And [so that] Allah may aid you with a mighty victory.”

The word عَزِيزًا does not merely mean “mighty” in the sense of powerful. The root ع-ز-ز carries connotations of honor, dignity, and unassailable authority. This is victory that commands respect, that cannot be dismissed or diminished.

Consider what happened in the year following Hudaybiyyah. When the Muslims returned for Hajj the next year, they came in such overwhelming numbers that there was no need for military confrontation. The Quraysh, who had broken the treaty through the actions of their allied tribes, could mount no effective response. Abu Sufyan (RA)—before his acceptance of Islam—rushed to Madinah attempting to renegotiate, only to be rebuffed. When he went to the home of his own daughter, Umm Habibah (RA), who was married to the Prophet ﷺ, she would not even allow him to sit on the Prophet’s ﷺ mat, declaring that as a mushrik, he was not worthy of it.

The conquest of Makkah itself fulfilled the Prophet’s ﷺ dream in precise detail. He had seen himself entering Makkah peacefully for pilgrimage. Had the Muslims engaged in battle at Hudaybiyyah, even if victorious, the dream would not have been fulfilled exactly as shown. The divine wisdom ensured that when the Muslims finally entered Makkah, they did so in such numbers and with such authority that resistance became impossible. This was نَصْرًا عَزِيزًا—a victory that came with complete authority and dignity, one that transformed enemies into allies without the bitterness that prolonged warfare creates.

Ayah 4

هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ ٱلسَّكِينَةَ فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ لِيَزْدَادُوٓا۟ إِيمَـٰنًا مَّعَ إِيمَـٰنِهِمْ ۗ وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا

“It is He who sent down tranquility into the hearts of the believers that they would increase in faith along with their [present] faith. And to Allah belong the soldiers of the heavens and the earth, and ever is Allah Knowing and Wise.”

Now Allah ﷻ addresses the emotional and spiritual state of the believers themselves. The Companions (RA) who accompanied the Prophet ﷺ to Hudaybiyyah were experiencing intense frustration. They had left Madinah fully prepared to make pilgrimage, ready to sacrifice their lives if necessary. When they were turned back and a treaty was signed that seemed to favor their enemies, their disappointment ran deep.

Notice the precise language Allah ﷻ uses: أَنزَلَ ٱلسَّكِينَةَ فِى قُلُوبِ , “sent down tranquility into the hearts.” The typical construction would be أَنزَلَ عَلَى, to send down upon. But Allah ﷻ says فِى , into. This is not tranquility poured over them like rain on the surface; this is tranquility injected directly into their hearts, penetrating to the depths of their frustration.

To understand why this depth was necessary, consider an analogy from medicine. Some ailments can be treated with topical cream applied to the skin. Others require injection, going deep beneath the surface to where the real problem lies. The Companions’ (RA) distress was not superficial—it was rooted in their deep love for the Ka’bah, their commitment to fulfill their religious duty, and their natural human desire to see immediate results. Surface-level comfort would not suffice. Allah ﷻ had to reach into the very core of their beings and plant tranquility there.

The phrase لِيَزْدَادُوٓا۟ إِيمَـٰنًا مَّعَ إِيمَـٰنِهِمْ , “that they would increase in faith along with their [present] faith,” reveals something extraordinary about the nature of faith itself. The Companions (RA) already possessed strong īmān—they would not have risked their lives to travel to Makkah otherwise. Everyone in Madinah thought they were marching to their deaths. It took genuine faith to make that journey.

But now Allah ﷻ tells them that their loyalty to the Prophet ﷺ in this difficult moment, their acceptance of his decision to return to Madinah despite their disappointment, represents a new dimension of faith. This is faith built on trust even when you cannot see the wisdom, faith that submits to divine decree even when it contradicts your desires, faith that respects the Prophet’s ﷺ judgment even when it seems to go against immediate success. This special īmān would be added to the īmān they already possessed.

Why did they need this tranquility? Not because of bad weather, not because they feared the Quraysh militarily. They needed tranquility specifically to accept the Prophet’s ﷺ decision to return. That was the source of their turmoil, and that was what Allah ﷻ addressed directly.

The ayah then shifts to a declaration of divine power: وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ , “And to Allah belong the soldiers of the heavens and the earth.” This is not merely a statement of fact; it is a direct message to the believers: You are part of My army, and when I command My army to advance, they advance. When I command them to withdraw, they withdraw. Your temporary retreat is not weakness—it is strategic repositioning under divine command.

Furthermore, Allah ﷻ is reminding them that they are not His only force. The armies of the skies and the earth are at His disposal. Dealing with the Quraysh is nothing for the One who commands such forces. Your role is to trust and obey, understanding that you are one component of a much larger operation.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا , “And ever is Allah Knowing and Wise.” The use of both these attributes together is significant. ’Alīm (Knowing) assures them that Allah ﷻ has complete knowledge of every variable, every consequence, every hidden factor that they cannot see. Hakīm (Wise) assures them that His plan is not just based on knowledge but on perfect wisdom—that He has chosen the optimal course of action from among all possibilities. Trust Allah’s ﷻ knowledge of the situation, and trust the wisdom of His plan.

Ayah 5

لِّيُدْخِلَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا وَيُكَفِّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا

“[And] that He may admit the believing men and the believing women to gardens beneath which rivers flow to abide therein eternally and remove from them their misdeeds - and ever is that, in the sight of Allah, a great attainment.”

The structure of these verses is remarkable. Notice that Allah ﷻ continues using لِ , “so that,” connecting each purpose back to the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. But here we see something striking: Allah ﷻ mentions both ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ , “the believing men and the believing women.”

This dual mention appears consistently throughout the Quran in contexts where we might not expect it. In Surah Al-Ahzab, during the discussion of the Battle of the Trench—a military engagement—Allah ﷻ spoke about both men and women. In Surah Muhammad, again in a military context, both are mentioned. Now in Surah Al-Fath, discussing a treaty and its aftermath, Allah ﷻ deliberately includes the women.

Why is this significant? Because while the men physically went to Hudaybiyyah, the women in Madinah faced their own trial. As the men departed, the hypocrites and their women engaged in vicious propaganda. They told the wives and mothers and daughters of the believers: “Your men are never coming back. They’re as good as dead. You might as well sell their belongings now and prepare for widowhood.”

The women endured this psychological warfare, this attempt to break their resolve and shake their faith. They had to maintain their trust in Allah ﷻ and their confidence in the Prophet’s ﷺ mission while surrounded by those who mocked and scorned them. Their test was different from their men’s test, but it was no less significant.

When the men returned victorious—not with military conquest but with a strategic treaty that would lead to ultimate triumph—the women’s faith was vindicated. Allah ﷻ acknowledges their parallel struggle by promising them the same reward: gardens beneath which rivers flow, eternal residence therein, and the removal of their misdeeds.

The phrase وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا shifts the believers’ perspective on success itself. You are looking for success in this world—the conquest of Makkah, the defeat of your enemies, the purification of the Ka’bah. These are worthy goals. But what you should truly be seeking is فَوْز —success in the sight of Allah ﷻ, success that extends beyond this temporary life into eternity. That is the ultimate victory, and that is what awaits both the men and women who remained faithful through this trial.

Ayah 6

وَيُعَذِّبَ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَٱلْمُشْرِكَـٰتِ ٱلظَّآنِّينَ بِٱللَّهِ ظَنَّ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۚ عَلَيْهِمْ دَآئِرَةُ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۖ وَغَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَعَنَهُمْ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ وَسَآءَتْ مَصِيرًا

“And [that] He may punish the hypocrite men and hypocrite women, and the men and women who associate others with Allah - those who assume about Allah an assumption of evil nature. Upon them is a misfortune of evil nature; and Allah has become angry with them and has cursed them and prepared for them Hell, and evil it is as a destination.”

Just as Allah ﷻ mentioned both believing men and women together, He now mentions both hypocritical men and women, and both mushrik men and women together. This parallelism is deliberate and instructive. In the propaganda campaign against Islam, in the spreading of doubt and fear, women played significant roles alongside men. They were not passive bystanders but active participants in attempting to undermine the believers’ morale.

The phrase ٱلظَّآنِّينَ بِٱللَّهِ ظَنَّ ٱلسَّوْءِ , “those who assume about Allah an assumption of evil nature,” captures the core of their error. What was their evil assumption? That Allah ﷻ would not grant victory to His Messenger ﷺ, that Islam would be crushed, that the believers who went to Hudaybiyyah were marching to their destruction. They assumed that Allah’s ﷻ promise was false, that His power was insufficient, that His wisdom was flawed.

This is why their punishment is described in such severe terms. Allah ﷻ lists five distinct consequences:

First, عَلَيْهِمْ دَآئِرَةُ ٱلسَّوْءِ , “Upon them is a circle of evil.” The very calamity they predicted for the believers would instead encircle them. As the sphere of Islamic influence expanded with each passing day following Hudaybiyyah, as each domino fell leading toward the conquest of Makkah, those who had mocked and spread fear found themselves increasingly trapped. The earth began to squeeze around them, their options narrowing, their allies abandoning them.

Second, وَغَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ , “And Allah has become angry with them.” Divine anger is not like human anger—it is perfectly just, measured, and purposeful. But it carries weight beyond human comprehension.

Third, وَلَعَنَهُمْ , “And has cursed them,” removing them from His mercy.

Fourth, وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَهَنَّمَ , “And prepared for them Hell.”

Fifth, وَسَآءَتْ مَصِيرًا , “And evil it is as a destination.”

Why such emphatic, repeated condemnation? There is profound psychological wisdom here. The believers were angry—angry at the treaty’s apparent unfairness, angry at being prevented from their pilgrimage, angry at the taunts of the hypocrites. This anger was natural and, to a degree, justified. But Allah ﷻ is teaching them: You think you are angry? You are not nearly as angry as I am at those who mock My religion and spread evil assumptions about Me. Your anger should subside in light of My anger. I have this situation completely under control.

This is another form of instilling سَكِينَة in their hearts. When someone you love shares your anger but to a greater degree, it often has a calming effect. If two siblings are fighting and a parent becomes even more upset at the wrongdoer than the wronged child is, that child often finds their own anger dissipating, even feeling a twinge of sympathy for the one being scolded. Allah ﷻ takes the believers’ righteous anger upon Himself, allowing them to release it and find peace.

Ayah 7

وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا

“And to Allah belong the soldiers of the heavens and the earth. And ever is Allah Exalted in Might and Wise.”

This declaration appears again, but notice that it stands as a separate ayah this time. When Allah ﷻ mentioned His armies in Ayah 4, it was connected to the believers in the same verse—because the believers are part of Allah’s ﷻ army. They belong in the same ayah with the mention of divine forces.

But here, after describing the hypocrites and the mushrikeen, Allah ﷻ mentions His armies in a separate, distinct ayah. They do not deserve to be mentioned in the same breath as the soldiers of Allah ﷻ. They are separate from His forces, outside His protection, excluded from His ranks. This separation is itself a statement of their rejection and exclusion.

The attributes chosen here are also significant: عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا , “Exalted in Might and Wise.” عَزِيز emphasizes Allah’s ﷻ unassailable authority and power to execute His plan. He has the might to punish those who opposed Him and to protect His believers. حَكِيم reminds us that all of this—the treaty, the apparent setback, the ultimate victory—is part of a wise plan that we must trust even when we cannot fully comprehend it.

Ayah 8

إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ شَـٰهِدًا وَمُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا

“Indeed, We have sent you as a witness and a bringer of good tidings and a warner.”

At first reading, this declaration might seem unnecessary. The Muslims already know that the Prophet ﷺ is a witness over humanity, that he brings glad tidings, that he warns of divine consequences. Why repeat this now?

The answer lies in understanding the psychological and spiritual impact of what the believers just experienced. They went to Hudaybiyyah ready to fulfill their religious obligation of pilgrimage. They were prepared to fight if necessary, to sacrifice everything for the sake of reaching the House of Allah ﷻ. When they were turned back and a treaty was negotiated, their sense of mission became confused. What were they doing? What was the purpose of this journey if not pilgrimage or victory?

Allah ﷻ reorients them to the fundamental mission. The Prophet ﷺ is sent as شَـٰهِد , a witness—someone whose life and character testify to the truth of Islam, who will stand witness over his Ummah on the Day of Judgment. He is مُبَشِّر , one who brings good news—the news of Paradise, of Allah’s ﷻ mercy, of ultimate success for those who believe. He is نَذِير , a warner—alerting people to the consequences of disbelief, calling them back from the edge of destruction.

These roles are not confined to Makkah or Madinah, to war or peace, to success or setback. They are constant. The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah did not change the Prophet’s ﷺ fundamental mission. The believers’ loyalty to him in that difficult moment should be understood in light of this mission. Their journey was not in vain because the witnessing continued, the good news continued, the warning continued.

This re-introduction to the Prophet’s ﷺ mission is like pressing a reset button on their relationship with him. After being tested so severely, after having their faith challenged and refined, they are ready to understand his role at a deeper level. They can now follow him not just in moments of apparent success, but in moments of apparent setback, trusting that the witnessing, the good news, and the warning continue regardless of circumstances.

The parallel with Surah Al-Ahzab is worth noting. Just as the Battle of the Trench could not have happened without the groundwork laid earlier, the conquest of Makkah could not have happened without Hudaybiyyah. These surahs are intimately connected, showing us how Allah ﷻ builds victory upon victory, how apparent defeats become the foundation for ultimate triumph, how every trial is purposeful in the divine plan.

Ayah 9–15 · linked video: 03. Al-Fath (Ayah 9-15)

Surah Al-Fath: Ayahs 9-15

Ayah 9

لِّتُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَتُعَزِّرُوهُ وَتُوَقِّرُوهُ وَتُسَبِّحُوهُ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا

This ayah addresses all Muslims collectively - “so that you all” can believe in Allah ﷻ and His messenger ﷺ. The phrasing suggests a renewal of faith, as if to say: refresh your iman in Allah ﷻ and His messenger ﷺ.

From here forward, the ayah establishes three critical aspects of our relationship with the Prophet ﷺ.

The first is تُعَزِّرُوهُ - to help him out of respect. This word captures a specific kind of assistance, where respect motivates action. Picture this: when a teacher walks in carrying heavy suitcases, what do you do? You immediately take them from his hands saying, “Let me carry that for you.” That’s ta’zir - helping someone purely out of honor and reverence for them. Whatever the Prophet ﷺ needs accomplished, we support him with complete respect. This becomes a foundational principle of our relationship with him, because Allah ﷻ sent him as a witness against all humanity, to give good news and to warn. Our work of dawah, our motivation to learn and teach the Quran - one of its primary drivers should be sheer respect for what our messenger ﷺ did.

The second aspect is وَتُوَقِّرُوهُ , from the root و-ق-ر , meaning seriousness and gravity. The word وِقْر refers to a burden, and تَوْقِير describes someone who possesses gravitas - a magnificent, weighty personality. When you’re in their presence, you’re overwhelmed by the grandeur of who they are. Some people carry such presence that being around them makes you think, “I can’t believe I’m actually here with this person.” That’s tawqir.

The classical scholars explain that wiqr also refers to a weight on your chest. When you’re in the presence of someone deserving tawqir, you feel a heaviness - not oppressive, but dignifying - that compels you to be your absolute best. Your behavior becomes more careful, more respectful. To help illustrate this concept: I once had an Algerian friend whose young child was crying at the masjid. I picked up the child to comfort him, but my friend immediately told me to put him down. When I asked why, he explained their family structure. Eight brothers live together with their wives and children under one roof, all under the authority of their father - the grandfather of all these children. With so many people living together, conflicts are inevitable. Children fight, women disagree, men argue. But the grandfather established a rule: if I come home and anyone is fighting - any child, any woman, any man - I will line everyone up and discipline the entire family. So throughout the day, there might be disputes and tensions, but at 5 PM when the father walks through the door, everything becomes calm. “How was your day?” “Everything’s fine.” What do they have for that grandfather? Tawqir. Whatever personal issues exist, his presence commands a different standard of behavior. That’s tawqir.

Now notice the remarkable connection Allah ﷻ makes: وَتُسَبِّحُوهُ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا - “and glorify Him morning and evening.” Tasbih - declaring the perfection of Allah ﷻ - is exclusively for Allah ﷻ alone. Yet in this ayah, showing respect and honor to the Prophet ﷺ becomes directly tied to declaring Allah’s ﷻ perfection. When you demonstrate this level of respect and gravity for the messenger ﷺ, you are engaging in the tasbih of Allah ﷻ in the morning and evening.

Ayah 10

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُبَايِعُونَكَ إِنَّمَا يُبَايِعُونَ ٱللَّهَ يَدُ ٱللَّهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۚ فَمَن نَّكَثَ فَإِنَّمَا يَنكُثُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِمَا عَـٰهَدَ عَلَيْهُ ٱللَّهَ فَسَيُؤْتِيهِ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا

The previous ayah presented the theory of respect and loyalty to the Prophet ﷺ. This ayah shows the practice.

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُبَايِعُونَكَ - “Those who pledge allegiance to you.” What pledge? The oath to avenge Uthman (RA), when the companions believed he had been killed by the Quraysh. The word بَيْعَة comes from بَيْع , meaning to sell. When the companions placed their hands in the Prophet’s ﷺ hand under the tree at Hudaybiyah, they were literally saying: I’m selling my life to you. I’m ready to die. This pledge is known by three names: Bay’at al-Ridwan (the Pledge of Allah’s Pleasure), Bay’at al-Mawt (the Pledge of Death), and Bay’at al-Shajarah (the Pledge of the Tree).

Then Allah ﷻ elevates the entire event: إِنَّمَا يُبَايِعُونَ ٱللَّهَ - “They are only pledging allegiance to Allah.” They showed loyalty to you, the Prophet ﷺ, but in reality, they showed loyalty to Me.

يَدُ ٱللَّهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ - “The Hand of Allah is above their hands.” In the physical act of bay’ah, you extend your hand and place it upon the hand of the one you’re pledging to. The Prophet ﷺ extended his hand, and the companions placed theirs on top. But Allah ﷻ declares: My Hand is above theirs. The honor belongs to Me.

فَمَن نَّكَثَ فَإِنَّمَا يَنكُثُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ - “Whoever violates the treaty, he only violates it against himself.” Here’s something profound to consider: did this pledge actually need to be fulfilled? No, because Uthman (RA) wasn’t killed. The entire situation was resolved, and the oath became nullified. Yet Allah ﷻ still says that whoever violates the oath only harms himself. Why? Because the oath isn’t merely about Uthman (RA). It’s about loyalty, respect, and honor. It’s the commitment: I will be with you no matter what happens. Anyone who goes back on that commitment only damages themselves.

The oath is with the messenger ﷺ, but Allah ﷻ clarifies: this is actually an oath you’ve taken with Me. The pledge of allegiance is with Allah ﷻ Himself.

وَمَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِمَا عَـٰهَدَ عَلَيْهُ ٱللَّهَ - “And whoever fulfills what he has promised Allah.” Notice the unique Arabic grammar here. In English, we would say “whoever fulfills whatever he promised Allah.” But in Arabic, the structure repeats the object: مَا عَـٰهَدَ عَلَيْهُ - “whatever he promised upon it (Allah).” Normally, Arabic follows phonetic rules where certain sounds are elided. We don’t say عَلَى الله, we say عَلَيْهِ (upon Him). But in this ayah, Allah ﷻ says عَـٰهَدَ عَلَيْهُ ٱللَّهَ - the pronoun هِ is followed immediately by the explicit mention of Allah ﷻ. This breaks the typical pattern because the kasrah (the “i” sound) cannot carry the heavy lam of the word Allah. The grammar itself has to become heavier, irregular, to accommodate the weight of the oath. This grammatical heaviness indicates to believers how serious and weighty an oath taken with Allah ﷻ truly is.

فَسَيُؤْتِيهِ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا - “Then He will give him tremendous compensation.”

Ayah 11

سَيَقُولُ لَكَ ٱلْمُخَلَّفُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ شَغَلَتْنَآ أَمْوَٰلُنَا وَأَهْلُونَا فَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَنَا ۚ يَقُولُونَ بِأَلْسِنَتِهِم مَّا لَيْسَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ ۚ قُلْ فَمَن يَمْلِكُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا إِنْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ ضَرًّا أَوْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ نَفْعًۢا ۚ بَلْ كَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًۢا

The hypocrites who stayed behind when the believers traveled to Hudaybiyah now face an awkward situation. They thought going to Makkah was foolish, that the Muslims would be slaughtered. But now the believers are returning more victorious than anyone imagined, having secured an unprecedented treaty. The hypocrites need to explain why they didn’t go.

Remember, the journey to Hudaybiyah was voluntary. Between 1,400 and 2,000 companions made the trip - entirely by choice. But the Sahaba understood something critical: when the Prophet ﷺ asks for something voluntary, it’s actually a test of iman. So massive numbers volunteered to go.

سَيَقُولُ لَكَ ٱلْمُخَلَّفُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ - “Those who were left behind from among the Bedouins will say to you.” The word مُخَلَّفُون is the passive form - those who were held back, left behind. In the story of the three companions whose repentance was delayed after the Battle of Tabuk, two of their camels were described as مُخَلَّف - forcibly left behind. This grammatical form indicates divine intervention. Earlier in Surah Al-Fath, Allah ﷻ said He held them back, telling them to sit with those who sit. Allah ﷻ divinely ordained that they should not have the honor of accompanying those with whom He would be pleased. He’s cleaning the ranks.

شَغَلَتْنَآ أَمْوَٰلُنَا وَأَهْلُونَا - “Our wealth and our families preoccupied us.” They come with ready excuses: “My business was extremely busy at that time. It was tax season - I hadn’t filed the forms yet. My wife’s family had come to visit, and you know how that gets. There was just so much going on in my life at that time.”

فَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَنَا - “So ask forgiveness for us.” Why would hypocrites care about the Prophet’s ﷺ dua? Because if people see the Prophet ﷺ publicly making dua for someone, that person’s reputation as a legitimate, loyal Muslim becomes established. They’re not coming because they genuinely want istighfar - they’re coming to look good in front of others, to maintain appearances that the Prophet ﷺ still considers them trustworthy.

But Allah ﷻ revealed this ayah before the Muslims even reached Madinah. “Soon, when you arrive in Madinah, the losers will come to you saying they were busy and asking you to pray for them.” When the hypocrites showed up with their rehearsed excuses - “We were so busy with family, could you please make dua for us?” - the ayah was recited. They arrived exactly as Allah ﷻ had predicted.

Notice how Allah ﷻ doesn’t even call them believers. They’re just موكلون من الأعراب - “those left behind from the Bedouins.” They’re dismissed as inconsequential.

يَقُولُونَ بِأَلْسِنَتِهِم مَّا لَيْسَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ - “They say with their tongues what is not in their hearts.” Here’s a beautiful linguistic detail. Earlier in the surah, when describing the aggressive speech of the hypocrites before Hudaybiyah, Allah ﷻ used the word أَفْوَاه (mouths): “They say with their mouths what is not in their hearts.” Mouths - the bigger organ, indicating they were running their mouths, talking loud, being aggressive. Remember their bold words? “If they had been with us, they wouldn’t have been killed.” “Don’t we have any authority? Nobody listens to us!” They had a mouthful to say.

But now, as they come with humble excuses, Allah ﷻ says أَلْسِنَة (tongues) - the smaller organ. They’re not yelling anymore. They’re submissive, quiet: “We’re so sorry. We got so busy. We didn’t even realize.” The shift from “mouths” to “tongues” perfectly captures their change in demeanor from arrogant opposition to meek excuse-making.

قُلْ فَمَن يَمْلِكُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا - “Say: Then who has any power for you against Allah?” The Prophet ﷺ is told to respond: Will my dua help you against Allah ﷻ? I cannot assist you before Him.

إِنْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ ضَرًّا أَوْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ نَفْعًۢا - “If He intends harm for you or intends benefit for you.” Notice harm is mentioned first, because for these hypocrites, harm seems more imminent.

بَلْ كَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًۢا - “Rather, Allah has always been All-Aware of what you do.”

Ayah 12

بَلْ ظَنَنتُمْ أَن لَّن يَنقَلِبَ ٱلرَّسُولُ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِيهِمْ أَبَدًا وَزُيِّنَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ وَظَنَنتُمْ ظَنَّ ٱلسَّوْءِ وَكُنتُمْ قَوْمًۢا بُورًا

Allah ﷻ exposes what was truly in their hearts.

بَلْ ظَنَنتُمْ أَن لَّن يَنقَلِبَ ٱلرَّسُولُ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِيهِمْ أَبَدًا - “Rather, you were convinced that the Messenger and the believers would never return to their families, ever.” They were certain this was the end. The Quraysh would annihilate them, and it would be finished.

وَزُيِّنَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ - “And this was made attractive in your hearts.” The passive voice indicates this beautification came from Shaytan. That idea had been decorated and beautified in their hearts. Abdullah bin Ubayy, the chief hypocrite, was already redesigning his throne, already rethinking how to restructure Madinah’s governance. “The Prophet ﷺ is gone now, so we need to plan how things will run around here.” He was preparing for his inauguration as the new leader of the city. Then the actual leader walks back in. Imagine his reaction.

وَظَنَنتُمْ ظَنَّ ٱلسَّوْءِ - “And you assumed the worst assumption.” I hope they get slaughtered. I hope it’s over quickly. All manner of evil thoughts ran through their minds, and Allah ﷻ recorded even those thoughts.

وَكُنتُمْ قَوْمًۢا بُورًا - “And you were a people headed towards destruction.” The word بُور (bur) refers to something declining slowly toward complete ruin. It’s like a business that gradually goes down until it’s finally bankrupt. Allah ﷻ didn’t annihilate this group of hypocrites immediately. He took them on a slow decline toward their eventual destruction.

Ayah 13

وَمَن لَّمْ يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ فَإِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ سَعِيرًا

The ayah follows immediately after the hypocrites came saying, “Make dua for us.”

وَمَن لَّمْ يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ - “And whoever does not believe in Allah and His Messenger.” Allah ﷻ cuts through their false presentation. No, you people don’t believe in Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ.

فَإِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ سَعِيرًا - “Then indeed, We have prepared for the disbelievers a blazing Fire.” Why does Allah ﷻ specifically say “disbelievers” here? Because He knows what’s truly inside their hearts. Allah ﷻ revealed what was in the hearts of the believers at the beginning of this surah. Now He reveals what’s in the hearts of the hypocrites, and He finds only kufr.

Ayah 14

وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا

وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ - “And to Allah alone belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth.” After exposing the hypocrites’ true beliefs and their ultimate fate, Allah ﷻ reasserts His absolute sovereignty.

يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ - “He forgives whom He wills, and He punishes whom He wills.” His mercy and His justice are both manifestations of His complete authority.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا - “And Allah has always been Forgiving, Merciful.” Even after describing the blazing Fire prepared for disbelievers, Allah ﷻ concludes by emphasizing His attributes of forgiveness and mercy. The door to repentance remains open for those who sincerely turn back.

Ayah 15

سَيَقُولُ ٱلْمُخَلَّفُونَ إِذَا ٱنطَلَقْتُمْ إِلَىٰ مَغَانِمَ لِتَأْخُذُوهَا ذَرُونَا نَتَّبِعْكُمْ ۖ يُرِيدُونَ أَن يُبَدِّلُوا۟ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قُل لَّن تَتَّبِعُونَا كَذَٰلِكُمْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ فَسَيَقُولُونَ بَلْ تَحْسُدُونَنَا ۚ بَلْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا

Those who stayed behind are about to open their mouths again.

سَيَقُولُ ٱلْمُخَلَّفُونَ إِذَا ٱنطَلَقْتُمْ إِلَىٰ مَغَانِمَ لِتَأْخُذُوهَا - “Those who remained behind will say when you set out toward spoils to take them.” The word انطَلَقْتُمْ ( means to march forward, to be released. It comes from طَلَاق - release, freedom. A husband and wife are bound together; when that bond is released, it’s called talaq. In modern Arabic, إطلاق also means to fire a gun, because the bullet is released.

The Muslims were held back from fighting at Hudaybiyah. But now, because of the treaty, they’ve been released. The bond preventing them from military action has been cut.

Notice what Allah ﷻ calls the upcoming confrontation: مَغَانِمَ - spoils, easy pickings. The word غَنِيمَة comes from غَنَم - sheep. Specifically, it refers to sheep you’re walking by and suddenly notice: “Hey, I’ll take that.” You find wealth or livestock with no apparent owner and you claim it. That’s ghanimah - something you just pick up off the ground and keep because no owner appears. The plural is maghanim.

Allah ﷻ is describing the upcoming Battle of Khaybar. Khaybar was a massive fort inhabited by powerful Jewish tribes. But Allah ﷻ doesn’t even call it a battle. He says it’s just maghanim - easy pickings, like finding an ownerless sheep. It won’t even be a fight. You’re just going there to pick up wealth.

The hypocrites hear about this. They used to collaborate extensively with the Jewish tribes. Now those ties have been severed by the Treaty of Hudaybiyah, and the Muslims are heading toward Khaybar - some of the most fertile, lush land in the region, full of gardens and valuable property. When the Muslims take control, significant assets will fall into their hands. The hypocrites calculate: “This isn’t like fighting the Quraysh. We know these Jews - they’re not skilled fighters anyway. We can take them on, beat them easily, and then get our share of the spoils.”

ذَرُونَا نَتَّبِعْكُمْ - “Let us follow you.” “We couldn’t come with you to Hudaybiyah because of family - you know, in-laws, you understand how that is. But now that you’re heading to Khaybar, can we come?” They’re practically begging to join.

But the Prophet ﷺ tells them no, they cannot come.

يُرِيدُونَ أَن يُبَدِّلُوا۟ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ - “They want to change the word of Allah.” The verdict of Allah ﷻ has already been issued: they don’t get to participate. They forfeited their right by abandoning the believers at Hudaybiyah.

قُل لَّن تَتَّبِعُونَا كَذَٰلِكُمْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ مِن قَبْلُ - “Say: You will never follow us. Thus has Allah said before.” This isn’t the Prophet’s ﷺ personal decision. Allah ﷻ declared this ruling before the situation even arose.

فَسَيَقُولُونَ بَلْ تَحْسُدُونَنَا - “Then they will say: Rather, you envy us.” When told they cannot join, they’ll accuse the believers of jealousy, of not wanting to share the spoils.

بَلْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا - “But they do not understand except very little.” They understand nothing. They fail to grasp that their absence from Hudaybiyah - their refusal to stand with the believers in hardship - disqualified them from sharing in the ease. They cannot comprehend the spiritual reality of what occurred under that tree, the divine pleasure that descended upon those who pledged their lives. Their understanding is superficial, limited to material gain. They see maghanim and want a piece. But the opportunity for that honor has passed.

Ayah 15–24 · linked video: 04. Al-Fath (Ayah 15-24)

Surah Al-Fath: Ayahs 15-24

Ayah 15

سَيَقُولُ ٱلْمُخَلَّفُونَ إِذَا ٱنطَلَقْتُمْ إِلَىٰ مَغَانِمَ لِتَأْخُذُوهَا ذَرُونَا نَتَّبِعْكُمْ ۖ يُرِيدُونَ أَن يُبَدِّلُوا۟ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قُل لَّن تَتَّبِعُونَا كَذَٰلِكُمْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ فَسَيَقُولُونَ بَلْ تَحْسُدُونَنَا ۚ بَلْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا

Allah ﷻ addresses the reality of those who remained behind during the Treaty of Hudaybiyah. When the promised spoils of war become imminent, these same people who refused to accompany the believers will say, “Let us follow you.” They want to participate now that there’s gain to be had, though they weren’t willing to share in the sacrifice and uncertainty.

Allah ﷻ reveals their true intention: يُرِيدُونَ أَن يُبَدِّلُوا۟ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ - they want to change the word of Allah ﷻ. This is profound because Allah ﷻ had already decreed who would participate in this particular expedition and who would not. By insisting on joining now for the spoils, they’re attempting to override divine decree itself.

The Prophet ﷺ is commanded to tell them directly: “You will not follow us.” This isn’t the Prophet’s ﷺ decision or personal judgment - كَذَٰلِكُمْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ مِن قَبْلُ - “This is what Allah ﷻ said from way before.” The matter was already decided by divine wisdom. Those who were held back were meant to be held back.

When faced with this rejection, they resort to accusations: “You’re just jealous of us. You don’t want us to have anything good. You think we’re good fighters, but you don’t want us to succeed. You don’t treat all Muslims equally. Our rights are being taken away.” This is classic deflection - rather than acknowledge their failure to answer the call when it mattered, they blame others for envy and unfairness.

Allah ﷻ cuts through their excuses with devastating clarity: بَلْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا - “But the fact of the matter is, they don’t understand anything except very little.” All their complaints and accusations stem from fundamental spiritual blindness. They can’t grasp the wisdom in what happened or understand the test they failed.

Ayah 16

قُل لِّلْمُخَلَّفِينَ مِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ سَتُدْعَوْنَ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ أُو۟لِى بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ تُقَـٰتِلُونَهُمْ أَوْ يُسْلِمُونَ ۖ فَإِن تُطِيعُوا۟ يُؤْتِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَجْرًا حَسَنًا ۖ وَإِن تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ كَمَا تَوَلَّيْتُم مِّن قَبْلُ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا

Now Allah ﷻ addresses these same Bedouins who stayed behind with a stark reality: “Soon you will be called to fight a nation أُو۟لِى بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ - possessing tremendous military might.” This time, He ﷻ doesn’t mention easy victories or abundant spoils. This won’t be like Khaybar where the enemy locked themselves in a fort until they ran out of supplies. This will be a nation with military capability and weaponry unlike anything they’ve seen before.

The reference here is to the Romans - the superpower that these very Bedouins were terrified of. You wanted to join the easy victory? You’ll get your chance, but it won’t be easy. You’ll be invited to face the Roman Empire itself.

The ayah presents two outcomes: تُقَـٰتِلُونَهُمْ أَوْ يُسْلِمُونَ - “You will fight them, or they will surrender.” Victory is assured, but it won’t come without genuine sacrifice and commitment. This is the test that really matters.

Then comes the beautiful promise: فَإِن تُطِيعُوا۟ يُؤْتِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَجْرًا حَسَنًا - “If you obey at that time, Allah ﷻ will give you tremendous compensation.” You want gardens? You want rewards? They’re available, but through real obedience and genuine sacrifice, not opportunistic participation when the path seems easy.

The warning is equally clear: وَإِن تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ كَمَا تَوَلَّيْتُم مِّن قَبْلُ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا - “And if you turn away as you turned away before, He ﷻ will punish you with a painful punishment.” You turned away from Hudaybiyah, and that was bad enough. But if you turn away again when called to face real hardship, the consequences will be severe.

Ayah 17

لَّيْسَ عَلَى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْأَعْرَجِ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ ۗ وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ يُعَذِّبْهُ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا

When the previous ayah warned about turning away bringing painful punishment, the sincere believers became concerned. Even those with legitimate excuses began to worry: “Do I have to go too? Should I prepare myself for battle?” The blind companions, the impaired companions, the elderly companions - all started making preparations to fight if called upon.

Allah ﷻ immediately clarifies: لَّيْسَ عَلَى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ حَرَجٌ - “There is no blame on the blind.” وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْأَعْرَجِ حَرَجٌ - “Nor on the impaired.” وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ - “Nor on the sick.” The word حَرَج means there’s no restriction, no problem, no burden placed upon them if they stay back. They are exempt.

This is the beautiful balance of Islamic law - accountability is tied to capability. Those with genuine limitations aren’t held to the same physical standards, but they’re still part of the believing community.

Then comes the principle that applies to everyone: وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ - “And whoever obeys Allah ﷻ and His ﷺ messenger” - in whatever capacity they’re able, whether they can fight or not, whether they’re healthy or impaired - يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ - “He ﷻ will enter them into gardens beneath which rivers flow.”

The flip side: وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ يُعَذِّبْهُ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا - “And whoever turns away, He ﷻ will punish them with a painful punishment.” This isn’t about physical capability. This is about the heart’s willingness to submit to Allah’s ﷻ command in whatever way one is able.

Ayah 18

۞ لَّقَدْ رَضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ يُبَايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ ٱلشَّجَرَةِ فَعَلِمَ مَا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَنزَلَ ٱلسَّكِينَةَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَثَـٰبَهُمْ فَتْحًا قَرِيبًا

This ayah is why Surah Al-Fath is also known as Bay’at ar-Ridwan - the Pledge of Allah’s ﷻ Pleasure. لَّقَدْ رَضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - “Indeed, Allah ﷻ was pleased with the believers” - إِذْ يُبَايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ ٱلشَّجَرَةِ - “at the very moment they were pledging allegiance to you under the tree.”

The tree becomes significant here because it marks the location of this historic pledge. The companions (RA) were so devoted that later generations wanted to venerate this tree, to visit it and pray extra prayers there. But this would have led to shirk - associating sanctity with objects rather than actions. That’s why the tree eventually had to be cut down. Even Ibn Abbas (RA), the great scholar and cousin of the Prophet ﷺ, would later say he couldn’t identify exactly where it was, ensuring people wouldn’t make it an object of worship.

What made this moment so special? فَعَلِمَ مَا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ - “He ﷻ knew what was in their hearts.” Allah ﷻ saw their sincerity, their willingness to die for the truth, their complete submission. They weren’t there for spoils or glory. They were there because they believed.

Because of what was in their hearts, فَأَنزَلَ ٱلسَّكِينَةَ عَلَيْهِمْ - “He ﷻ sent down tranquility upon them.” In earlier ayahs, when the believers were frustrated by the terms of the treaty, tranquility had to enter their hearts from within. Now, having proven their sincerity, tranquility is poured onto them from above - a divine gift for their unwavering faith.

The reward? وَأَثَـٰبَهُمْ فَتْحًا قَرِيبًا - “And He ﷻ rewarded them with a near victory.” This is Khaybar, the conquest that would come soon after Hudaybiyah.

Ayah 19

وَمَغَانِمَ كَثِيرَةً يَأْخُذُونَهَا ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا

This ayah defines what that “near victory” means: وَمَغَانِمَ كَثِيرَةً يَأْخُذُونَهَا - “And abundant spoils of war that they will take.” The word يَأْخُذُونَهَا suggests they’ll simply take them without tremendous effort. This is describing Khaybar, where the Jewish tribes who had violated their treaty with the Muslims locked themselves in their fortress until they ran out of supplies and had to surrender.

When they emerged, the Prophet ﷺ asked them how they wanted to be judged. They chose to be judged according to their own scripture and selected Abdullah bin Salam (RA), a companion who had been a rabbi before accepting Islam. They likely thought he would go easy on fellow former Jews. Instead, he opened the Torah and ruled that when a nation commits treachery against another with whom they had a treaty, all their men capable of fighting should be executed. They had asked for judgment by their own book, and that judgment was carried out.

The ayah concludes: وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا - “And Allah ﷻ has always been the Almighty, the All-Wise.” His ﷻ might and wisdom were both on display - might in granting victory, wisdom in the circumstances that led to it.

Ayah 20

وَعَدَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ مَغَانِمَ كَثِيرَةً تَأْخُذُونَهَا فَعَجَّلَ لَكُمْ هَـٰذِهِۦ وَكَفَّ أَيْدِىَ ٱلنَّاسِ عَنكُمْ وَلِتَكُونَ ءَايَةً لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا

Allah ﷻ now puts Khaybar in a larger context: وَعَدَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ مَغَانِمَ كَثِيرَةً تَأْخُذُونَهَا - “Allah ﷻ has promised you many spoils of war that you will take.” There will be many victories ahead, many conquests, much reward for the believers.

فَعَجَّلَ لَكُمْ هَـٰذِهِۦ - “But He ﷻ has hastened this one for you” - Khaybar was expedited, brought forward as an immediate reward for their patience at Hudaybiyah.

But there’s another dimension to this victory: وَكَفَّ أَيْدِىَ ٱلنَّاسِ عَنكُمْ - “And He ﷻ has restrained the hands of other people from you.” The Quraysh had a secret alliance with the Jewish tribes. When the Muslims moved against Khaybar, the Quraysh could have seen it as an opportunity to attack Medina or the Muslim forces. But Allah ﷻ held their hands back. The Treaty of Hudaybiyah, which seemed like a setback, actually prevented the Quraysh from coming to their allies’ aid.

This is describing Hudaybiyah itself - the first clear victory was that the hands of these people were tied. What seemed like a concession was actually strategic brilliance.

وَلِتَكُونَ ءَايَةً لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - “And so that it may become a clear sign for the believers.” The whole sequence of events - the treaty, the restraint, the quick victory - all of it serves as evidence of Allah’s ﷻ power and wisdom for those who believe.

وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ صِرَٰطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا - “And so He ﷻ may guide you to a straight path.” Even in victory, the purpose is guidance. Success isn’t just about winning battles; it’s about being led along the path that pleases Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 21

وَأُخْرَىٰ لَمْ تَقْدِرُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا قَدْ أَحَاطَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرًا

After mentioning Khaybar, Allah ﷻ speaks of another conquest: وَأُخْرَىٰ لَمْ تَقْدِرُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا - “And another that you have not yet been able to achieve.” This is referring to Makkah itself, though it’s not spelled out explicitly yet.

قَدْ أَحَاطَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا - “Allah ﷻ has already surrounded it.” The city is already within His ﷻ control, already encompassed by His ﷻ decree. The conquest is guaranteed; it’s just a matter of time. The sphere of Muslim influence keeps expanding, and Makkah’s fall is inevitable.

Notice how casually this is mentioned - it’s just “another one of those victories.” Not because it’s insignificant, but because with Allah ﷻ on their side, even the conquest of their hometown, the center of opposition, the heart of idol worship, is already assured.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرًا - “And Allah ﷻ has always been, over all things, completely capable.” This is a statement of absolute divine power. Whatever He ﷻ wills will come to pass. The believers’ job is to trust and work; the outcome is already in His ﷻ hands.

Ayah 22

وَلَوْ قَـٰتَلَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوَلَّوُا۟ ٱلْأَدْبَـٰرَ ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا

This ayah addresses what would have happened if events had unfolded differently: وَلَوْ قَـٰتَلَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ - “And if those who disbelieve had fought you” - meaning at Hudaybiyah or afterward - لَوَلَّوُا۟ ٱلْأَدْبَـٰرَ - “they would have turned their backs and fled.”

The phrase وَلَّوُا۟ ٱلْأَدْبَـٰرَ literally means turning one’s back, running away in defeat. This is exactly what happened to small factions of Makkans who did try to engage the Muslims after the conquest of Makkah. They attempted resistance, but they couldn’t sustain it.

ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا - “Then they would find neither protector nor helper.” Once defeated, they’d be completely abandoned. No ally would come to their aid, no support would materialize. This was the reality after Makkah fell - those who continued to resist found themselves isolated and eventually had to be expelled from the land.

This ayah confirms that the restraint at Hudaybiyah wasn’t due to Muslim weakness. If fighting had broken out, the disbelievers would have been routed. The peace was purely by Allah’s ﷻ wisdom and decree.

Ayah 23

سُنَّةَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِى قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَلَن تَجِدَ لِسُنَّةِ ٱللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا

Allah ﷻ now universalizes this principle: سُنَّةَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِى قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلُ - “This is the Sunnah of Allah ﷻ that has been in effect since before.” The word سُنَّة here means His ﷻ established way, His ﷻ consistent pattern in dealing with nations.

This isn’t new. Throughout history, when disbelieving nations oppose the truth with violence and tyranny, Allah ﷻ destroys them. When believing communities face overwhelming opposition, He ﷻ grants them victory. This is His ﷻ legacy, His ﷻ pattern that has played out again and again.

وَلَن تَجِدَ لِسُنَّةِ ٱللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا - “And you will never find any change in the Sunnah of Allah ﷻ.” This is absolute. His ﷻ ways don’t change based on time period, technology, or circumstances. The principles remain constant. Truth will prevail over falsehood. Oppression will be answered. Patience will be rewarded. This is how Allah ﷻ operates, and it’s immutable.

This ayah provides tremendous comfort to believers in every era. When facing opposition, when truth seems weak and falsehood seems strong, remember: Allah’s ﷻ pattern never changes. What He ﷻ did for previous believers, He ﷻ will do for those who hold firm to faith.

Ayah 24

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى كَفَّ أَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ عَنْهُم بِبَطْنِ مَكَّةَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَنْ أَظْفَرَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرًا

The surah returns to describe the miraculous restraint at Hudaybiyah in more detail: وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى كَفَّ أَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ عَنْهُم - “And He ﷻ is the One who restrained their hands from you and your hands from them.”

This was a two-way restraint. The Quraysh were held back from attacking, and the Muslims were held back from retaliating. Both sides were on the brink of violence, but Allah ﷻ held the situation together.

بِبَطْنِ مَكَّةَ - “In the belly of Makkah” - right in the heart of the city, in the most volatile location possible. The Muslims were making pilgrimage to their sacred city, now controlled by their enemies. The tension was extraordinary. One wrong move could have ignited catastrophic violence.

مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَنْ أَظْفَرَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ - “Even after He ﷻ had given you the claws to snatch them with.” The word أَظْفَرَ comes from ظُفْر (nail or claw), used when a predator catches its prey. When a tiger grabs with its claws, when a hawk snatches a rabbit - this is ظَفَر.

Allah ﷻ is telling the believers directly: You had them. You were the superior force. You were the claw about to catch the victim. I know I told you to make peace and go back, but don’t think it was because you were weak. You had the upper hand. The only one who prevented that violence from erupting was Me.

This is Allah ﷻ honoring His ﷻ faithful companions, acknowledging their strength even as He ﷻ commanded restraint. It wasn’t the Prophet ﷺ alone who stopped the fighting - it was divine intervention.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرًا - “And Allah ﷻ was seeing everything you were doing.” He ﷻ watched their discipline, their obedience, their restraint despite having the power to win. He ﷻ saw what was in their hearts - the frustration, the desire for justice, but ultimately the submission to His ﷻ command. And He ﷻ rewarded that submission with the greatest victory of all: the eventual conquest of Makkah without bloodshed, the triumph of truth without devastating loss of life.

This is the beauty of Surah Al-Fath - what seemed like defeat was actually victory, what felt like humiliation was actually honor, and what appeared to be retreat was actually the first step toward ultimate triumph.

Ayah 25–28 · linked video: 05. Al-Fath (Ayah 25-28)

Surah Al-Fath: Ayahs 25-28

Ayah 25

هُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَٱلْهَدْىَ مَعْكُوفًا أَن يَبْلُغَ مَحِلَّهُۥ ۚ وَلَوْلَا رِجَالٌ مُّؤْمِنُونَ وَنِسَآءٌ مُّؤْمِنَـٰتٌ لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوهُمْ أَن تَطَـُٔوهُمْ فَتُصِيبَكُم مِّنْهُم مَّعَرَّةٌۢ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۖ لِّيُدْخِلَ ٱللَّهُ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ لَوْ تَزَيَّلُوا۟ لَعَذَّبْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا

Allah ﷻ declares unequivocally: they are the ones who disbelieved, and they are the ones who prevented all of you from Al-Masjid Al-Haram. The criminals are them, not you. The Muslims had committed no crime by attempting to make Hajj. This is Allah’s commentary about the events of Hudaybiyyah.

Then Allah ﷻ adds: وَٱلْهَدْىَ مَعْكُوفًا (and they kept the hady detained). The hady refers to the sacrificial animals. The animals marked for sacrifice at Hajj are called hady. The word مَعْكُوفًا means “held back” or “detained.” It comes from the same root as i’tikaf, where a person stops themselves from leaving the confines of the masjid. These animals were prevented from reaching their destination: أَن يَبْلُغَ مَحِلَّهُۥ (that it could reach the place of sacrifice).

Consider the significance of this. The Muslims hadn’t simply walked from Medina to Makkah. They brought these animals for a sacred purpose. During tough economic times, they had to feed these animals throughout the journey across the desert, enduring the hardship and expense, only to discover at the end that they wouldn’t be able to sacrifice them as intended.

But then Allah ﷻ reveals a profound wisdom: وَلَوْلَا رِجَالٌ مُّؤْمِنُونَ وَنِسَآءٌ مُّؤْمِنَـٰتٌ (had it not been for believing men and believing women). Notice that both men and women are mentioned explicitly, though the mention of men would typically include women. Why this emphasis? Because these believers are مَّعْرِفُون (unknown) - they’re anonymous, unidentified.

There were people in Makkah at that time who were closet Muslims. They had embraced Islam after the believers made hijrah, but the Companions (RA) didn’t know about them. These individuals had heard the message, reflected deeply on it, and concluded in their hearts that they were Muslim. Yet they couldn’t reveal their faith because war had broken out, and exposing their Islam would put them in grave danger. They were essentially held hostage in Makkah, unable to declare their iman publicly.

If war had erupted, the Muslims wouldn’t have recognized them as fellow believers. They would have appeared as potential enemies, and in the chaos of battle, innocent Muslims could have been killed. Allah ﷻ says: لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوهُمْ أَن تَطَـُٔوهُمْ (whom you did not know, lest you trample them).

The consequence? فَتُصِيبَكُم مِّنْهُم مَّعَرَّةٌۢ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ (and you would be afflicted by scandal from them without knowledge). An embarrassing stigma would have attached itself to the Muslim community. Eventually, the truth would emerge. When the families of these secret believers revealed that their loved ones were actually Muslim, a devastating narrative would spread: Muslims don’t even spare their own in battle. A people who speak of tribal bonds can’t even respect the bonds of religion. They claim to follow the way of God, yet they kill their own people.

If we read these ayahs carefully today, we see their painful relevance. How many Muslims are being killed by Muslims in our world?

Allah ﷻ explains His wisdom: لِّيُدْخِلَ ٱللَّهُ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ (so that Allah may admit into His mercy whom He wills). Those hidden believers deserved Allah’s mercy too. The prevention of war wasn’t just about the Companions (RA) receiving tranquility and reward as part of a greater plan. There was another crucial benefit: these secret Muslims could survive until Makkah was conquered. They would live to declare their shahada openly and publicly, and they would have the honor of seeing the Messenger of Allah ﷺ face to face. They would be counted among the Companions (RA). Had war broken out then, they would have been denied this tremendous blessing.

Then Allah ﷻ states: لَوْ تَزَيَّلُوا۟ لَعَذَّبْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (Had they been apart, We would have punished those who disbelieved among them with painful punishment). The word تَزَيَّلُوا۟ means to slip away, to move from one’s place, to separate. If those believing men and women had somehow managed to sneak out of Makkah, then the disbelievers among them - the Makkans - would have faced Allah’s painful punishment through annihilation.

This reinforces a principle we find throughout the Quran: Allah ﷻ does not destroy a town while there are still people within it seeking His forgiveness. The Makkans hadn’t been annihilated precisely because that small group of believers remained, making istighfar in secret.

Ayah 26

إِذْ جَعَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلْحَمِيَّةَ حَمِيَّةَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِيَّةِ فَأَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُۥ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ وَعَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَأَلْزَمَهُمْ كَلِمَةَ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ وَكَانُوٓا۟ أَحَقَّ بِهَا وَأَهْلَهَا ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمًا

Allah ﷻ now describes the psychological state of the disbelievers: إِذْ جَعَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلْحَمِيَّةَ (when those who disbelieved had put into their hearts al-hamiyyah).

The word الْحَمِيَّةَ requires careful examination. It comes from الحمى , a term used in classical Arabic for protected territory. When a king owned camels, horses, sheep, or cattle, he wouldn’t let his animals graze with the common people’s livestock. He would fence off vast acres exclusively for his royal animals - a special, elite space where ordinary people couldn’t even step foot. This designated territory was called hima.

From this root comes hamiyyah: an intense, protective pride, an attitude of exclusivity and superiority. It’s the mindset of someone at an elite country club looking down at an ordinary person trying to enter: “What are you doing here? This isn’t your place. You’re not worthy of being here.”

Interestingly, Islam transformed the meaning of this word. In a famous incident, Umar ibn Al-Khattab (RA) went out with a servant on an intensely hot day. From the shade where they were resting, they spotted a man in the middle of the desert, herding two camels under the scorching heat. As the man approached, they realized it was Umar (RA) himself on another occasion. When asked why he would venture out in such extreme conditions, Umar (RA) explained that two camels from the sadaqah collection had wandered off, and he went to retrieve them to return them to al-hima.

What is hima in Islamic terminology? The place where the camels of sadaqah graze. The protected territory is no longer the king’s private stable - it’s the pasture for charity animals, for those creatures whose milk, meat, and skins will be distributed to the poor and needy. This is the Islamic redefinition of privilege and protection.

But here, Allah ﷻ speaks of حَمِيَّةَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِيَّةِ - the zealotry of ignorance. This is tribal pride, nationalist arrogance, racist superiority - all forms of group-based supremacy rooted in ignorance rather than truth. When people possess this jahili pride, they talk down to others, they disrespect, they provoke.

Picture the scene: the Quraysh confronting the Muslims with this arrogant attitude, and on the other side, young men holding swords. What happens when one gang mentality meets another? When the Quraysh stepped forward with their hamiyyah, it could easily have triggered a counter-reaction. Blood versus Crips. One tribe against another. One ethnic group versus another. This is the cycle of hamiyyat al-jahiliyyah.

But Allah ﷻ intervened: فَأَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُۥ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ وَعَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (so Allah sent down His tranquility upon His Messenger and upon the believers). Here again, Allah mentions sakinah. When the disbelievers were stepping up with arrogance, getting in their faces, creating a volatile situation on top of the already tense treaty negotiations, why didn’t the Muslims retaliate? Only because Allah sent down tranquility upon them.

The treaty had just been made. It was a close call that could have erupted into violence. Now the Quraysh were being provocative again, and it required divine intervention to maintain peace.

وَأَلْزَمَهُمْ كَلِمَةَ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ (And He committed them to the word of taqwa). The word أَلْزَمَ means to stick something onto something else, like a permanent sticker. Allah stuck the word of taqwa into their hearts so firmly that they couldn’t remove it. Everywhere they looked, all they could see was: we must have taqwa of Allah.

وَكَانُوٓا۟ أَحَقَّ بِهَا وَأَهْلَهَا (And they were more deserving of it and worthy of it). They were the most deserving of the word of taqwa, and they were its people. What is this “word of taqwa”? It’s the Quran itself. Allah committed them to the Quran, and they are deserving of the Quran, and they are its people.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمًا (And Allah is Knowing of all things). Allah ﷻ knew exactly what the believers were feeling. He knew the impulse of the Companion who thought, “I should knock this guy out for the way he’s talking to us, for the way he’s looking at the Prophet ﷺ.” Can you imagine the self-control required of Umar ibn Al-Khattab (RA) and others as they watched this scene unfold? Allah knew what they were enduring, and He understood their struggle to hold themselves back.

Ayah 27

لَّقَدْ صَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ رَسُولَهُ ٱلرُّءْيَا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ لَتَدْخُلُنَّ ٱلْمَسْجِدَ ٱلْحَرَامَ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ءَامِنِينَ مُحَلِّقِينَ رُءُوسَكُمْ وَمُقَصِّرِينَ لَا تَخَافُونَ ۖ فَعَلِمَ مَا لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا۟ فَجَعَلَ مِن دُونِ ذَٰلِكَ فَتْحًا قَرِيبًا

Now imagine the Muslims heading back to Medina after Hudaybiyyah, holding in so much anger and disappointment. Allah ﷻ had calmed them down, but questions lingered. Someone dared to ask the Prophet ﷺ: “Didn’t you see a dream? Weren’t we supposed to perform Hajj?” The dream of a prophet, after all, is revelation.

The Prophet ﷺ never said it would happen that specific year. Then this ayah was revealed: لَّقَدْ صَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ رَسُولَهُ ٱلرُّءْيَا بِٱلْحَقِّ (Certainly has Allah fulfilled for His Messenger the vision in truth).

Allah ﷻ makes an emphatic declaration: لَتَدْخُلُنَّ ٱلْمَسْجِدَ ٱلْحَرَامَ (You will certainly enter Al-Masjid Al-Haram). The structure of this sentence in Arabic emphasizes absolute certainty - you will absolutely, definitely enter.

But then comes إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ (if Allah wills). Why does Allah ﷻ use “if Allah wills” when He’s making a guaranteed promise? When Allah speaks of His own actions, He doesn’t say “insha’Allah” about Jannah, for example, because that’s His established decree. But this future event depends on the Muslims’ behavior. The promise is guaranteed, but your part in it requires proper conduct. The victory is very close, but you Muslims must maintain your character and behavior. If you change, then even now, Allah’s will can change accordingly.

The ayah continues with beautiful, specific details: ءَامِنِينَ (in safety). You will enter peacefully, securely, without fear. Remember, the Prophet ﷺ once led the Companions (RA) in Salat al-Khawf, the prayer of fear, when they weren’t safe. Now Allah promises they’ll enter without that fear.

مُحَلِّقِينَ رُءُوسَكُمْ وَمُقَصِّرِينَ (with your heads shaved and hair shortened). This describes the casual, normal scene of Hajj. Some will shave their heads, others will trim their hair. Notice that shaving is mentioned before shortening, indicating that shaving is the better of the two options. The point is: you won’t be afraid at all. You’ll be performing the regular rituals of Hajj in complete security.

فَعَلِمَ مَا لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا۟ (For He knew what you did not know). Allah ﷻ knew all along the benefits of not entering Makkah at that time. He knew the wisdom in returning. He knew how this would open doors. He knew about the treaties that would be made as a result. He knew that entering Makkah for war was impossible then, but it could only become possible because of Hudaybiyyah.

فَجَعَلَ مِن دُونِ ذَٰلِكَ فَتْحًا قَرِيبًا (And He has arranged before that a near victory). Besides the eventual entry into Al-Masjid Al-Haram, Allah installed for you something else to keep you engaged - a nearby victory, an immediate conquest to ease the tension and demonstrate that Allah’s plan was already unfolding.

Ayah 28

هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُۥ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلدِّينِ كُلِّهِۦ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًا

The surah concludes with a powerful declaration of purpose: هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُۥ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ (It is He who sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth).

The Prophet ﷺ was sent with بِٱلْهُدَىٰ - ultimate, complete guidance. And with دِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ - the religion of truth, justice, and purpose. The word الْحَقِّ carries multiple profound meanings:

First, it means truth - that which is real and undeniable. Second, it means purpose - that which has genuine meaning and direction. Third, it means rights and justice - that which gives everyone what they deserve. A father gives his son what he deserves, a son gives his father what he deserves, we give the Messenger ﷺ what he deserves, the Messenger gives us what we deserve. Everyone’s rights are honored and fulfilled. This is fundamental to our religion. And ultimately, we strive to give Allah ﷻ His rights, though we can never fully repay what He deserves.

The purpose? لِيُظْهِرَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلدِّينِ كُلِّهِۦ (That He may make it prevail over all religion). The word يُظْهِرَ means to make dominant, to overshadow, to make manifest and supreme. This din of guidance and truth will prevail over all other ways of life, all other lifestyles, all other systems of judgment.

This ayah tells us that one aspect of the Prophet’s mission was the conquest of Makkah. No other religious system would remain in Arabia. The only way of life that would endure there would be Islam. The direct implication is that the Messenger ﷺ was sent to cleanse Arabia of all false religions, leaving only Islam.

وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًا (And sufficient is Allah as Witness). Allah Himself bears witness to this mission and its fulfillment. He is enough as a witness to the truth of this promise.

This is the second time this ayah appears in the Quran. The first was in Surah At-Tawbah, and here it appears again in Surah Al-Fath. It will appear a third time in Surah As-Saff. Each context adds layers of meaning to this declaration of Islam’s ultimate triumph through the mission of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

Ayah 29 · linked video: 06. Al-Fath (Ayah 29)

Ayah 29

مُّحَمَّدٌ رَّسُولُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥٓ أَشِدَّآءُ عَلَى ٱلْكُفَّارِ رُحَمَآءُ بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ تَرَىٰهُمْ رُكَّعًا سُجَّدًا يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنًا ۖ سِيمَاهُمْ فِى وُجُوهِهِم مِّنْ أَثَرِ ٱلسُّجُودِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مَثَلُهُمْ فِى ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ ۚ وَمَثَلُهُمْ فِى ٱلْإِنجِيلِ كَزَرْعٍ أَخْرَجَ شَطْـَٔهُۥ فَـَٔازَرَهُۥ فَٱسْتَغْلَظَ فَٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَىٰ سُوقِهِۦ يُعْجِبُ ٱلزُّرَّاعَ لِيَغِيظَ بِهِمُ ٱلْكُفَّارَ ۗ وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ مِنْهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًۢا

This remarkable ayah comes after Allah ﷻ has described the opening of Makkah and the promise that the believers will enter Al-Masjid Al-Haram. He has established that the Messenger ﷺ was sent to give this religion dominance. But now Allah ﷻ reveals the how—what are the means by which this goal will be accomplished? Just as Musa (AS) was told to go to Firaun and was given the staff and signs to aid him in his mission, this ayah reveals what Allah ﷻ has given Muhammad ﷺ as the means to achieve victory.

The Companions as the Prophet’s Greatest Asset

The ayah begins with a powerful declaration: مُّحَمَّدٌ رَّسُولُ ٱللَّهِ—Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah ﷻ. Then immediately follows: وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥٓ—and those who are with him. The word مَعَ (with) here carries immense significance. Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “those who believe in Muhammad” or simply “the believers.” He says those who are with him. Being with the Messenger ﷺ means something far deeper than mere belief. It means that his mission has become your mission. His cause has become your cause. His worries have become your worries. When you say “I am with you,” you’re saying, “Whatever you’re dealing with, I’m dealing with. Your problem is my problem.”

This is like the bond within a family—when a son looks upset, the mother becomes upset. When the husband is troubled, the wife shares that concern. The companions who were truly مَعَ (with) the Prophet ﷺ had reached this level of commitment. Umar (RA) would later say, “أنا رسول رسول الله—I am the messenger of Allah’s Messenger,” seeing himself as a complete extension of the prophetic mission.

This understanding helps us grasp an essential reality: our religion consists of multiple dimensions. Islam is a religion with obligations to fulfill. It is a constitution with a framework for governance and society. And it is a cause—a mission that demands struggle and dedication. A religion simply asks you to pray five times, fast during Ramadan, perform hajj when able, and observe certain guidelines. But a cause brings people together to struggle for something greater, and they don’t stop until that cause is fulfilled.

We have largely reduced Islam to just the religious aspects. The constitutional framework—the collection and distribution of zakat through organized systems, the welfare rights guaranteed to certain segments of society—these have been abandoned. Before we can talk about implementing an Islamic state, we need to successfully implement these principles at the organizational level with just twenty people. Only then can these structures evolve naturally.

The Progressive Journey from Religion to Cause

The journey of understanding Islam unfolds in stages. First comes personal reform—you accept Islam as your religion and fulfill its basic obligations. No one will tell you it’s obligatory to study the entire Quran in translation, but as your heart develops, you find yourself wanting to study it. When you begin this study, you discover that most of the Quran isn’t focused on the technicalities of wudu or the details of divorce law. Rather, two-thirds of it is the Prophet ﷺ addressing the disbelievers, saying, “Listen, I’m here to save you.”

This is when you begin to see Islam differently. You stop viewing it merely as a religion and start recognizing it as a prophetic mission. As you progress further in your study, you realize that this mission isn’t over—you are part of it. You are someone meant to deliver this word on behalf of your Messenger ﷺ. This represents intellectual and spiritual progression. You undergo a personal religious transformation, then you begin seeing the religion as a cause, and finally you recognize yourself as a necessary component of that cause, someone who aids the Messenger ﷺ in his mission.

The sahaba had to progress through these stages, and they were asked to do far more than we will ever be asked to do. When the Prophet ﷺ said to fight, they fought. When he said to stop, they stopped. Today, we face confusion—some scholars say one thing, others say another. When you find yourself in such a confusing situation, the proper response is to take a step back, take a deep breath, and reassess everything. Look at the reality around you. How nuanced was Allah’s wisdom in the Treaty of Hudaybiyah? How cognizant was it of the realities on the ground? There were secret Muslims in Makkah that even the companions didn’t know about. There were hypocrites in Medina. So many complex realities were taken into consideration.

If Muslims are going to think about Islam as a cause, we must first understand the reality on the ground. The world changes rapidly—the world of twenty years ago is not the world of today. When circumstances change, strategy must change. When the world changes, our thinking must evolve. How we apply Islamic principles must evolve. The Prophet ﷺ kept a constant eye on changing situations—this was the genius of his leadership, and this is something we must inherit from him.

Two Sides of the Believer’s Character

The sahaba who were truly with the Prophet ﷺ are described with two fundamental characteristics. First: أَشِدَّآءُ عَلَى ٱلْكُفَّارِ—they are intense, firm, tough against the disbelievers. It’s crucial to understand that الْكُفَّار here doesn’t mean all non-Muslims. Most of humanity consists of non-believers, and Allah ﷻ doesn’t command us to be harsh with everyone who doesn’t accept Islam. Rather, كُفَّار refers specifically to those who actively oppose Islam, who detained Uthman (RA), who demonstrated the arrogance and tribalism of pre-Islamic ignorance, who prevented the believers from Al-Masjid Al-Haram.

These are the enemies of Islam—people who kill Muslims, who hate Muslims, who curse our religion and call it satanic. Against such people, we should not be apologetic. When they challenge us directly and attack our faith, we stand firm. This doesn’t contradict the call to dawah, but there’s a time for gentle invitation and a time for firm defense of truth.

The other side of the coin is described in the next phrase: رُحَمَآءُ بَيْنَهُمْ—merciful among themselves. Those truly with the Prophet ﷺ have the utmost love and respect for anyone who says “Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah ﷻ.” They are two sides of the same coin: tough against the enemies of Islam, yet full of mercy and compassion toward fellow believers.

The Inner Spiritual Reality

The ayah continues: تَرَىٰهُمْ رُكَّعًا سُجَّدًا—you see them in ruku and sujud. Then comes a beautiful phrase: يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنًا—they are pursuing favor from Allah ﷻ and His pleasure, His contentment. This describes their inner motivation. Everything they do, they do seeking the blessing and pleasure of Allah ﷻ.
